# Introduction and Background

Point estimation is a fundamental skill in the statistician's toolbox. Formally, we define a point estimator $\hat{\theta}(X) = \hat{\theta}(X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n)$ as any function of the sample $X$. We use such functions to obtain point estimates, $\hat{\theta}(x) = \hat{\theta}(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$, or any realized value from a point estimator. One might wonder how this applies to practical statistical analysis.


Often when conducting statistical analysis, we rarely have information regarding the entire population of interest. As such, we must rely on the available data to estimate the true population parameters. To do this, we compute statistics, or functions of the observed data such as the sample mean, and use them as our parameter estimates. Because statistics are functions of the sample, they are, by definition, point estimators.
